coredata.dc:title: Integrating biphase γ- and α-Fe2O3 with carbon dots as a synergistic nanozyme with easy recycle and high catalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shengliang
Zhang, Wenjing
Li, Ning
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
coredata.dc:description: A new method is offered for the synthesis of heteronanostructures as a multifunctional catalyst mimicking functions of dual- or multi-enzyme activities. In this method, the coordination reaction and surface adsorption are firstly used to preform ferrous and ferric ion-modified carbon dots (CDs) for regulating heterogeneous nucleation process of iron oxide. Thus a unique multiphase nanostructure containing CDs and biphase γ- and α-Fe2O3 is obtained under hydrothermal conditions. As-prepared heteronanostructure with magnetic properties shows excellent enzyme-like activities under visible light irradiation, which is more than a 6-fold enhancement compared to pure phase Fe2O3 nanomaterials. This could be attributed to highly efficient charge separation and transfer efficiency induced by heterogeneous interfaces as well as multiphase-induced new electronic energy level structure. Moreover, its catalytic activities can be also tuned by temperature, pH, hydrogen peroxide and levels of oxygenation in microenvironments. Not only does this work open an effective strategy to design novel heteronanostructures, but it also may provide a synergistic nanozyme as a multimodal platform for a broad of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Iron oxide
Photocatalysis
Magnetic heterostructure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the procedure for the synthesis of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3; (b) XRD patterns of samples; (c) IR spectra of samples; (d and e) XPS Fe 2p and O 1s spectra of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3.
(a and b) SEM images of α-Fe2O3; (c and d) SEM images of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3; (e and f) SEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of Fe, C, O, and N.
TEM and HRTEM images of α-Fe2O3 (a, b) and CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 (c, d).

UV–Vis spectra of TMB solution added with CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 (a, b) and α-Fe2O3 (d, e) versus different time under dark condition and light irradiation, respectively; and nonlinear fittings of Michaelis-Menten curves for TMB oxidation catalyzed by CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 (c) and α-Fe2O3 (f).

(a) The effects of initial concentration ratios of ferrous and ferric ions interacted with CDs on catalytic activities of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3; (b, c) The effects of temperature and pH on catalytic activities of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3; (d) The cycling stability of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3.
(a, b) EIS and XPS valence-band spectra of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3; (c, d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of NBT with irradiation time in the presence of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of TMB solution added with CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations; (b) The relationship of the oxidation product of TMB with H2O2 concentrations at the given reaction time.

head.ce:title: Integrating biphase γ- and α-Fe2O3 with carbon dots as a synergistic nanozyme with easy recycle and high catalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new method is offered for the synthesis of heteronanostructures as a multifunctional catalyst mimicking functions of dual- or multi-enzyme activities. In this method, the coordination reaction and surface adsorption are firstly used to preform ferrous and ferric ion-modified carbon dots (CDs) for regulating heterogeneous nucleation process of iron oxide. Thus a unique multiphase nanostructure containing CDs and biphase γ- and α-Fe2O3 is obtained under hydrothermal conditions. As-prepared heteronanostructure with magnetic properties shows excellent enzyme-like activities under visible light irradiation, which is more than a 6-fold enhancement compared to pure phase Fe2O3 nanomaterials. This could be attributed to highly efficient charge separation and transfer efficiency induced by heterogeneous interfaces as well as multiphase-induced new electronic energy level structure. Moreover, its catalytic activities can be also tuned by temperature, pH, hydrogen peroxide and levels of oxygenation in microenvironments. Not only does this work open an effective strategy to design novel heteronanostructures, but it also may provide a synergistic nanozyme as a multimodal platform for a broad of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes (termed as nanozymes) have high stability, low cost and unique nanomaterial properties, thereby they offer more advantages than natural enzymes in many applications [1–4]. By integrating the highly enzyme-like activity with the magnetic property, nanozymes are not only separated more economically and efficiently, but are also easily remotely-controlled by a magnetic field for in situ monitoring and tracking [1,2]. In 2007, magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles with peroxidase-like activity were firstly unveiled by Yan et al. based on a well-known phenomenon that Fe2+/Fe3+ ions as Fenton’s reagent in solution can decompose H2O2 [5]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles facilitated the oxidation of typical peroxidase substrates such as 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and diazo-aminobenzene (DAB) in the presence of H2O2 to induce colorimetric reactions. Comparing to Fe3O4 nanoparticles, but the other iron oxide (Fe2O3) nanomaterials showed poor peroxidase-like activity [6,7]. This could be attributed to the lack of ferrous iron available on their surfaces [6]. To improve their activity, an efficient route was to modify Fe2O3 nanomaterials with different amounts of Prussian blue and other nanospecies, such as SiO2, α-FeOOH, graphene oxide [6,8–11]. As a type of narrow band gap semiconductor, theoretically visible light energy can be also introduced to activate Fe2O3. However, there are well-known drawbacks including poor conductivity, light penetration depth, low charge carrier mobility and the fast recombination of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs for naked Fe2O3 [12,13]. As a result, Fe2O3 itself did not exhibit a high enzyme-like activity under light irradiation compared to other photoactive nanozymes.

Carbon dots (CDs) have a broad variety of potential applications in biomedical, energy, and environmental fields [14–17]. In particular, their excellent electron-accepting and -donating properties were endowed with a huge electron-storage capacity [18–20]. Thus, CDs enable the separation and transport of electrons and holes photoinduced in nanocomposites, causing the decrease of recombination probability in various kinds of defects [14,15,21–23]. Therefore, many CDs-based heterostructures or hybrid materials were developed recently for the use of light-driven catalytic synthesis and degradation [14–16]. CDs were combined with α-Fe2O3 to improve photocatalytic activity [19,24]. However, this composite was difficult to be separated and controlled in an external magnetic field due to lack of magnetic properties of α-Fe2O3. Although the γ-Fe2O3 nanostructures possess inherent magnetic properties [25], unfortunately so far the CDs-combined γ-Fe2O3 nanozyme is rarely reported owning to the need for stringent and intricate preparation processes. Moreover, the nanomaterials of α- or γ-Fe2O3 were often studied individually and scarce to be assembled into a heterostructure system for better charge separation [25].

In this contribution, we developed a facile approach to prepare biphase γ- and α-Fe2O3 on the surface of CDs (named as CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3) through the hydrothermal reaction. CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 exhibits pH-/temperature-dependent oxidase-like and/or superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities via light stimuli as well as high peroxidase-like activity. The catalytic activity of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 was beyond 6 times higher than that of α-Fe2O3 alone under visible light irradiation. Moreover, recycling use studies indicated that CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 has better stability than pure Fe2O3.

FeSO4·7H2O (0.3 mM) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.9 mM) were added into 30 mL of as-prepared CDs solution from our previous report [26]. The blending solution was stirring for 5 h and then the formed precipitations were collected by centrifugation. Subsequently, NaOH solution (1.0 M) was added into the precipitations drop by drop under stirring till pH value of the mixed solution reached to 11. The resultant solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, heated to and maintained at 200 °C for 4 h, and allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. Under-layer depositions were washed several times with ethanol and water alternately. After dried at 60 °C under vacuum, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 was obtain finally. For comparison, FeSO4·7H2O (0.3 mM) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.9 mM) were dissolved into water and adjusted to pH 11 using NaOH, then followed the same process with above method to obtain the other sample, i.e. α-Fe2O3.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max 2550 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The sample solution was dropped onto Cu grids covered with an amorphous carbon film to prepare specimen for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation which were performed in a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) observations were performed in a Hitachi S-4800 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples was collected by a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer having an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Fluorescence spectroscopy and absorption were collected on a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer, respectively.

TMB oxidation by CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 or α-Fe2O3 in pH buffer solution produced a blue signal with major absorbance peaks at 652 nm (its oxidation product, TMBox). The absorbance of the color reaction (at 652 nm for TMBox) was recorded at a certain light irradiation time using a UV-2550 UV–Vis. spectrometer. The visible light irradiation with an intensity of 100 mW·cm−2 was provided by a solar light simulator (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, China) with an optical cut-off filter that was utilized to remove wavelengths below 420 nm. The concentration of TMBox was obtained using the Lambertbeer's law: A = εbc, where A is the absorbance which can be measured; ε is the molar extinction coefficient (ε = 39,000 M −1 cm −1 for TMBox); b is the path length of light (b = 1 cm). The oxidation rates (V) were obtained by calculating the slopes of concentration changes of TMBox with time. V max and K m were obtained by fitting the data with the Michaelis−Menten equation: V = V max [S]/(K m + [S]), where [S] is the concentrations of substrates.

Electrochemical measurements were performed by a three-electrode electrochemical cell system. Working electrodes were fabricated by coating ethanol suspension of the samples onto indium tin oxide (ITO) glass electrodes, and then dried in a vacuum at 100 °C to evaporate the solvent. 0.5 M of Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte solution, and a platinum plate and Ag/AgCl reference electrode acted as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. All of the data were collected on a Bio-Logic (SP 120) electrochemical workstation.

The heterostructure of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 was obtained by controlling the reaction in the early stage of the heterogeneous nucleation. We chose our previously prepared CDs that can interact with Fe2+ and Fe3+ simultaneously as the heterogeneous nucleation seed [26]. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs adsorbed Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions enough were precipitated from solution. Subsequently, adding NaOH solution into the precipitation formed the new mixed solution. The resulting solution was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The precipitation was collected finally after removing the supernatant and washing several times by water and absolute ethanol to obtain CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. Since Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions were adsorbed on the surface of CDs, the nucleation reaction of iron oxide could preferentiallyoccur at CDs, allowing the synthesis of CDs-based heterostructures at the nanoscale.

Fig. 1b shows XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. It combines the standard pattern of maghemite γ-Fe2O3 (PDF#25-1402) and that of α-Fe2O3 (PDF#33-0664), confirming the coexistence of γ- and α-Fe2O3 in the sample. To compare, the control sample was prepared by the same method as that for CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 but in the absence of CDs. Its XRD pattern indicated that all of the diffraction peaks are coincided with the α-Fe2O3 (PDF#33-0664). This verifies that only α-Fe2O3 phase is produced in the absence of CDs. Unlike CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3, as-prepared α-Fe2O3 did not exhibit any magnetic response (Figure S1). Fig. 1c shows the infrared (IR) spectra of the above both samples. The IR spectrum of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 contains the vibration peaks of CO and C-N groups due to the presence of CDs. By contrast, these peaks are not observed in IR spectrum of pure α-Fe2O3 sample. Moreover, the surface chemical compositions of the samples were determined by XPS characterizations (Figure S2). As shown in Fig. 1d, the samples have similar Fe 2p XPS peaks that correspond fairlywell to Fe3+ in Fe2O3 [27]. However, the O 1s XPS spectrum of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 can be decomposed into three peaks assigning to Fe-O, CO and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e). Both CO and C-O bonds are derived from CDs in the CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3.

Fig. 2 shows SEM images of α-Fe2O3, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. From Fig. 2a and b, it can be observed that α-Fe2O3 displays a sheet-like structure with a large size. Differently, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 is composed of a lot of tiny particles (Fig. 2c and d). Elemental mapping for Fig. 2e illustrates the homogeneous distribution of Fe, O, C, N elements in CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3, demonstrating the formation of CDs-combined Fe2O3 heteronanostructure. The above results were further confirmed by TEM images. TEM image of α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 3 a) reveals sheet-like morphologies in accordance with SEM results. Additionally, the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm is observed from high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, which agrees with the (104) plane of α-Fe2O3. Fig. 3c displays the aggregation of small nanoparticle for CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. Furthermore, the amorphous carbon nanoparticle and its surrounding α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3 with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm corresponding to (119) plane are presented together in HRTEM image of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 3d). Therefore, the heteronanostructure composed of CDs, γ- and α-Fe2O3 was produced by our presented method.

To determine the ability of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 to behave as a nanozyme, we selected TMB as the oxidation substrate. For peroxidase-catalyzed reaction, H2O2 is commonly needed as the oxidizing agent or electron acceptor [4]. In our experiment, we did not add any H2O2. From Fig. 4 a, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 is able to promote the oxidation of TMB in buffer solution (pH = 3.5) on the dark condition, but it exhibits a low activity. Under visible light irradiation, Fig. 4b illustrates the significantly enhanced oxidation rate of TMB in the presence of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. The characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm of oxidation product of TMB (TMBox, blue color) was increased by about four times with light irradiation [5,28]. According to the different oxidation rates with variable concentrations of TMB under light irradiation, we obtain a hyperbola relationship complying with the Michiaelis-Menten equation after nonlinear Hill function simulation (Fig. 4c). Based on the obtained plot, the maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) defining the nanozyme activity was estimated. The obtained value of Vmax is 3.39 μM·min−1, which is much higher than that of other nanozymes reported previously (Table S1). For comparison, the activity of α-Fe2O3 was also performed under the same conditions. Fig. 4d displays UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB oxidation with time in the presence of α-Fe2O3 on the dark condition. Clearly, TMB was barely oxidized using α-Fe2O3 as the nanozyme without illumination. Under visible light irradiation, Fig. 4e depicts its relatively higher catalytic activity for TMB oxidation. However, its activity is still much lower than that of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. From typical Michaelis–Menten kinetics, we determined the Vmax of α-Fe2O3, which value is 0.556 μM·min−1 with illumination. As for the value of Vmax, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 is more than 6 times of that of α-Fe2O3.

Fig. 5 a show the effects of initial concentration ratios of ferrous and ferric ions interacted with CDs on the catalytic performance of as-prepared CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. The sample of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 with the highest catalytic activity for TMB oxidation was obtained at molar ratio of Fe2+:Fe3+ = 1:3. What’s more, we conducted the catalytic performances of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 while varying the temperature from 10 to 50 °C and the pH from 1 to 5 (Fig. 5b and c). The optimal temperature and pH are approximately 35 °C and pH 3.5. Such properties are very similar with those of the Fe3O4 nanozyme [5]. As for the oxidation of TMB, the activity of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 is almost unchanged after irradiating for 7 h under visible light intensity of 100 mWcm−2 (Figure S3). Since CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 can be conveniently separated fromthereaction systemusing a magnet, we also examined the cycling stability of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the catalytic efficiency of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 for TMB oxidation remains above 88% after5cycles, whereas that of α-Fe2O3 is only about 25% under the same conditions. By further XRD characterization, no obvious differences were observed from diffraction peaks before and after repeated photocatalytic cycling (Figure S4), suggesting better structural stability of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3.

The high stability of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 could be attributed to CDs. A broad of reports have demonstrated that CDs can play a crucial role in catalyticstability by means of their delocalized electrons, carbon vacancies or dangling bonds [21,23,29]. The superior catalytic activity of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 should result from its highly efficient charge transfer under visible light irradiation. To determine charge transfer efficiency, we estimated the interface impedance in samples through the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). Fig. 6 a illustrates that the arc radius of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 is only a half of α-Fe2O3, suggesting easier migration of photogenerated charges in CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. Besides, the abundant heterogeneous interfaces produced among different crystal structures could induce band edge banding that is beneficial to the separation of electron and hole pairs. All of above reasons ensure more photogenerated carriers to the surface for contributing catalytic reaction, leading to a high activity.

Under irradiation, electrons are injected from the oxidation nanocatalyst and react with oxygen to generate the oxidizing O2
- superoxide radical (E0, O2/O2
- = −0.046 ~ −0.33 eV) [30],
(1)
O 2
+
e
-
→
O 2
∙ -

Meanwhile, holes could react with HO– or H2O to form oxidative HO· (E0, HO·/HO– =2.38 eV − 0.059pH) [30],
(2)
H 2
O +
h
+
→
HO
∙
+
H
+
or
OH
-
+
h
+
→
HO
∙

Nevertheless, whether the reactions (1) and (2) can proceed or not depends on the energy level positions of conductor band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. Accordingly, the VB positions of as-prepared CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3 were ascertained by XPS valence-band spectra. From Fig. 6b, the VB positions of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3 are 1.88 and 2.24 eV, respectively. Comparing with the potential of HO·/HO–, the photoinduced reaction (2) can’t occur for CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 at pH = 3.5. Therefore, we deduced that the photoinduced reaction (1) could take place alone on CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3. To confirm it, we detected O2·- using nitro blue tetrazorium (NBT) with a characteristic absorption peak at around 258 nm. NBT can be reduced by O2·- to form formazan, leading to the decrease of characteristic absorption peak [30,31]. After irradiating for 10 min, the characteristic peak of NBT solution added with CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 disappears (Fig. 6c) while that added with α-Fe2O3 is almost unchanged (Fig. 6d). Both CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3 display similar light absorption features (Figure S5a). By the Tauc plots, their corresponding optical band gaps were determined to be 2.17 and 2.18 eV (Figures S5b and c), respectively, which are in agreement with the reported values (2.0–2.2 eV) of hematite [13]. Thereby α-Fe2O3 is difficult to induce the reaction (1) under visible light irradiation. This could be attributed to a very little of O2·- production in α-Fe2O3 after 10 min irradiation.

Based on the definition of oxidase (which enables substrates to oxidize employing O2 as the electron acceptor) [3,4], CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 can be categorized as an oxidase-like nanozyme though it shows low activity without light excitation and a photo-response oxidase-like nanozyme with high catalytic activity. Interestingly, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 can also possess peroxidase-like activity. As reveals in Fig. 7 a, its activity to catalytic oxidation of TMB markedly increases with the amount of H2O2. Nevertheless, the relationship of the oxidation rates of TMB with the amount of H2O2 is not linear and analogous to peroxidase performance (Fig. 7b). Accordingly, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 belongs to a peroxidase-like nanozyme, too. Since abundant O2·- radicals can be generated under light irradiation, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 may have an ability to disproportionate them into O2 and H2O2 to further promote TMB oxidation, i.e. it can mimic the behaviors of SOD [4]. Such multienzyme mimetic function of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 could endow it with high activity toward the oxidation of TMB.

A new method has been developed to promote the synthesis of CDs-incorporated γ- and α-Fe2O3 heteronanostructure. As-prepared CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 with magnetic properties shows excellent enzyme-like activities under visible light irradiation, which is more than a 6-fold enhancement compared to pure phase α-Fe2O3 nanomaterials. This could be attributed to highly efficient charge separation and transfer efficiency induced by heterogeneous interfaces as well as multicomponent-created new electronic energy level structure. Moreover, catalytic activities of CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 can be tuned by temperature, pH, light irradiation, H2O2 and levels of oxygen in microenvironments. The roles of CDs played in heteronanostructure are crucial. Not only do CDs facilitate the formation of magnetic γ-Fe2O3, but they also stabilize CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 catalytic activities. As a multifunctional nanozyme, CDs@γ-/α-Fe2O3 may be used as a multimodal platform with promising applications in biomedicine, environmental pollution abatement and clean manufacturing.

Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Wenjing Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Ning Li: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qing Chang: Writing - original draft, Validation. Jinlong Yang: Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082, 201803D421091), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.148987.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Chem. Soc. Rev.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

Nat. Nanotechnol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prussian blue modified iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles and their high peroxidase-like activity
J. Mater. Chem.

Nanotechnology

J. Hazard. Mater.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of α-FeOOH/γ-Fe2O3 by a pH gradient method and the role of γ-Fe2O3 in H2O2 activation under visible light irradiation
Chem. Eng. J.
